cards.body: Inom means 'drink.' Another root word. This one uses the um- pattern, like kain.
Um- goes inside: in-um-om = uminom. 'To drink' or 'drank.'

example.note: Same um- pattern as kumain. The um- goes after the first vowel when the word starts with a vowel.

cards.body: Tubig means 'water.' The most basic inom.

example.note: 'ng' (pronounced 'nang') connects the verb to what you drank.

cards.source_text: How do you say 'I drank water'?
cards.explanation: Uminom = drank. Tubig = water. Ng connects them.

cards.source_text: What's the root word of uminom?
cards.explanation: Inom = drink (root). um- infix turns it into uminom.

cards.body: You just saw 'ng' in action. It connects verbs to their objects. Kumain ng adobo. Uminom ng tubig. You'll see ng everywhere.

example.note: ng (pronounced 'nang') = of / connects verb to what you ate/drank.

cards.explanation: Uminom ako ng tubig = I drank water.
Inom uses um-, like kain. K-um-ain, um-inom.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: Ng is one of the most common words in Tagalog. It connects verbs to their objects and shows possession. You'll see it in almost every sentence.
Tubig is pure Tagalog. Many Filipino words for natural elements (tubig, hangin/wind, apoy/fire) predate any foreign influence.
In the Philippines, offering someone water (tubig) or food is the first thing you do when they visit. Hospitality isn't optional.
